Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to dedicate about 23 hours a week to studying, but with six online courses running simultaneously, I had to be strategic. I'm a hands-on learner, so I spent a lot of time doing practice problems and working through examples physically rather than just reading. I'd often study in chunks between my extracurricular activities, grabbing time whenever I could find it.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling about particular assignments. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Deadlines definitely drove my schedule more than any planned routine. When my anxiety was particularly high, I'd sometimes freeze up and then have to compensate later with intense study sessions.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if something was due soon or seeing if grades had been posted. Sometimes my peer support group would message me about something on the platform, and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't someone who just browsed the LMS casually—I needed a specific reason to be there. The anxiety of possibly missing something important was often what pushed me to check.
Q4: Honestly, it never really became routine for me. I logged in maybe twelve times over the course, which isn't much. When I did develop any habit, it was usually because my study group would coordinate through the platform and I'd check in to see what they were discussing. But I never got to the point where opening the LMS was automatic like checking my phone.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful—I watched over two hours worth because I could pause, rewind, and take physical notes while following along. That kinesthetic element of writing while watching helped things stick. I also found the discussion posts useful when peers explained concepts differently than the instructor. Having that peer support connection made some resources feel more accessible.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials that were just walls of text. Without something interactive or hands-on, I'd lose focus quickly. Some of the supplementary resources felt overwhelming when I was already anxious about the core material. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came because I was just trying to keep my head above water with the essentials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what would be assessed or if it helped reduce my anxiety about understanding the material. Anything that let me practice and do something with my hands rather than just passively consume information felt valuable. If my peers said something was helpful, I was more likely to prioritize it too.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. With my anxiety levels being what they are, I had to triage constantly. Required activities got done because the consequences of not doing them were concrete and scary. Optional ones, even if interesting, got pushed aside because I was already struggling to complete about half my assignments as it was.
Q9: Absolutely, this was actually my primary way of studying. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then work through them with a notebook, drawing diagrams and rewriting concepts by hand. I used physical flashcards a lot and would practice problems on paper. My textbooks were heavily highlighted and annotated—none of that shows up anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through practice problems at my desk or in study rooms with my peer group, and none of that registered on the LMS. I'd print materials once and study from those papers for weeks. My most productive study sessions often happened completely offline, especially when I needed to reduce screen time to manage my anxiety.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a video or document once and then refer back to it multiple times offline. It was actually better for my anxiety to have materials saved locally so I didn't have to worry about internet issues or constantly logging in. I'd watch downloaded lectures on my phone during commutes or between activities without any of that being tracked.
Q12: My anxiety would sometimes spike to the point where even opening the LMS felt overwhelming. When multiple deadlines converged or when I got feedback that wasn't what I hoped for, I'd shut down for a bit. There were weeks where my extracurricular commitments intensified and I just couldn't give the course the attention it needed. The low motivation made it hard to push through those moments.
Q13: Definitely. With six online courses, the workload was substantial, and confusion about priorities was constant. When I was fatigued, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because it felt like too much. My extracurriculars sometimes took precedence, especially when they provided the social support I needed to manage my anxiety. The LMS often got the short end of the stick during overwhelming periods.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere, usually spiraling about other deadlines or worrying about my performance. I'd technically be 'on' the platform but not absorbing anything meaningful. High anxiety made it hard to concentrate even when I was trying to engage. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments regularly, even important ones. My anxiety would make me avoid things I felt uncertain about—if I wasn't confident I could do something well, I'd put it off. The irony is that avoiding it made the anxiety worse. Sometimes I knew exactly what I needed to do but the thought of potentially failing paralyzed me into inaction.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage sometimes. I'd have bursts of activity right before things were due, cramming and submitting what I could. The structure they provided was actually helpful for someone with low motivation like me—without them, I might have drifted even more. But they also triggered my anxiety significantly.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback from peers or instructors would boost my mood and make me more likely to engage. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid the course for a while. The peer support intervention helped because feedback felt less threatening coming from fellow students.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat overwhelming with six courses running at once. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize, but the volume of content made it hard to keep up. I appreciated when things were organized logically, but I often felt like I was just trying to survive rather than thrive within the structure.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement easier because I could study in my preferred hands-on way. The discussion forums were helpful when active. What made it harder was when too much was crammed into one module or when instructions weren't clear—that triggered my anxiety and made me want to disengage. Video content was more accessible than dense readings for me.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually being able to settle down and produce work. You might log in multiple times, watch videos, read discussions—all trying to understand or calm your nerves—but then freeze when it comes to actually submitting something. The fear of doing it wrong can be paralyzing even when you're actively trying.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. I performed reasonably well on exams despite low LMS activity because I did most of my meaningful studying offline. I'd work through materials physically, study with peers in person, and use my textbook extensively. The LMS was just one small piece of how I actually learned. My exam score reflects learning that happened away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see that I was anxious and distracted while a video played, or that I spent three hours with printed notes and a study group. They capture clicks and time stamps, not understanding or effort. The human element of learning is invisible to them.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts, and feeling like the learning matters. For me, it also involves doing something active—writing, practicing, discussing with peers. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, not just showing up or logging in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my success, or how much studying I did with physical materials away from any screen. It wouldn't capture my anxiety levels or how much energy it took just to submit something despite feeling paralyzed by fear of failure. It also wouldn't show that despite low visible engagement, I was thinking about course content constantly, just processing it in my own kinesthetic way.
